impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity of Teva's headache patents under § 112 written description and enablement, reinstating the jury's findings of willful infringement by Eli Lilly. The case involves blockbuster migraine drug patents (Ajovy) and has major implications for the scope of patent protection for antibody-based therapeutics.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent protection for biopharmaceutical innovators claiming genus-level antibody patents, particularly where the specification discloses prior-art methods of making and humanizing antibodies. Supports broader patent claims in the biologics space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Increases risk for biosimilar and competing drug developers who may face broader patent infringement claims covering classes of therapeutic antibodies rather than only specifically disclosed molecules.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, even when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers exclusively purchasing Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products, which the jury found had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope). The court upheld the damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Reinforces that competitors can challenge patent licensing arrangements that leverage patent rights to monopolize markets for unpatented goods, protecting market access for competing product manufacturers.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring exclusive-purchase license agreements tying unpatented products to patent licenses, especially where the tied products have uses beyond the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to methods for detecting anomalous program executions using an emulator and a model of function calls are directed to an abstract idea under step one of the Alice § 101 framework, and whether damages for patent infringement may include sales to foreign customers.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents are abstract under Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that damages cannot include Norton's foreign sales. Additionally, the court vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees that were based in part on an improper contempt finding against Norton's counsel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from the court's reinforcement that claims directed to detecting anomalous software behavior via comparison to models may be patent-ineligible, and that foreign sales are excluded from domestic patent damages calculations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and patent holders asserting cybersecurity-related software patents face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges and cannot recover damages based on the accused infringer's foreign revenue.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content specifications to a wireless device's capabilities and delivers requested content are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents claiming server-side tailoring of content to device capabilities are patent-ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and a $2.5 million damages award against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to an abstract idea and required entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming platforms and technology companies gain protection against patent assertions covering routine server-side content adaptation, as such claims may be deemed abstract and patent-ineligible.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content optimization and adaptive streaming face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, potentially invalidating significant patent portfolios in the streaming technology space.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether there was legally sufficient evidence to support the jury's finding that plaintiffs' asserted trade secrets were protectable trade secrets under California law, and whether generally known information can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the jury's trade secret misappropriation verdict, finding insufficient evidence that the four asserted trade secrets were protectable under California law because key information was generally known. The court also reversed the patent invalidity finding, holding that contributions of generally known ideas cannot constitute inventive contributions for inventorship. The counterfeiting verdict was affirmed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses defending against trade secret claims benefit from the court's rigorous requirement that plaintiffs demonstrate each asserted trade secret is not generally known, raising the evidentiary bar for trade secret plaintiffs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies asserting trade secrets face a higher burden to prove protectability, particularly in industries where the claimed secrets overlap with publicly known techniques or general industry knowledge.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to selecting a processor core based on measured processing speed parameters require that the claimed acts occur within the United States for infringement purposes, and whether VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory was properly rejected.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed-in-part the district court's summary judgment of noninfringement of VLSI's multicore processor patent against Intel, finding that the extraterritoriality ground was improperly applied and remanding for further proceedings. The court affirmed the rejection of VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory but reversed the striking of VLSI's damages expert theories.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting claims against semiconductor and processor companies benefit from a narrower application of the extraterritoriality defense, allowing infringement claims to proceed where some claimed steps occur domestically.
impacts.negativeImplications: Chip manufacturers and technology companies face continued exposure to patent infringement claims for multicore processor technologies even where some operations occur outside the U.S.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify at summary judgment about matters outside personal knowledge, and whether computer source code constitutes hearsay under Federal Rule of Evidence 801.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the on-sale bar (pre-AIA § 102(b)), holding that a 30(b)(6) corporate witness may testify beyond personal knowledge for summary judgment purposes and that computer source code (as opposed to embedded human-written comments) is not hearsay. This allowed the court to rely on testimony about historical product sales and source code analysis to establish prior art.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in patent cases can more readily prove on-sale bar invalidity using 30(b)(6) corporate testimony and source code evidence, even when original engineers are unavailable, expanding the evidentiary tools for prior art challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased vulnerability to invalidity defenses based on decades-old product sales, as courts may allow corporate representative testimony about events predating the witness's tenure and admit source code as non-hearsay evidence.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (which had been granted for the patent challenger) and Walker Process antitrust fraud (which had been granted for the patent holder), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves coiled tubing patents in the oil and gas industry where the patent holder allegedly concealed prior art acquired through a corporate acquisition.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send inequitable conduct claims to trial where there is evidence of concealment of acquired prior art, creating accountability for patent prosecution conduct.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions face trial risk on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust claims if there is evidence suggesting concealment during prosecution.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Patent Trial and Appeal Board correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's obviousness determination should be sustained.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media encryption patent and vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge. The case was remanded for further proceedings under the corrected claim construction, potentially invalidating claims covering partial encryption of streaming content.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies benefit from a more precise construction of encryption-related patent claims, potentially narrowing or invalidating patents that broadly claim streaming encryption technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content protection space face risk that claim terms covering encryption will be construed more narrowly, making their patents more vulnerable to invalidity challenges.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices, and whether Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watches that infringed Masimo's wearable blood oxygenation patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing models. The court found no error in the domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the wearable health technology space are bolstered by the affirmance of ITC exclusion orders, confirming that innovative medical sensor technology can be effectively protected through trade enforcement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies importing products with health-monitoring features face significant import restrictions and business disruption from ITC exclusion orders when found to infringe medical device patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on the absence of a damages remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent case against Microsoft's Azure platform, holding the exclusion was an abuse of discretion. The court vacated the summary judgment that had been granted based solely on the absence of a remedy after the expert was excluded, emphasizing that damages methodology disputes should generally go to the jury.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for admitting damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that methodological disputes result in case-dispositive exclusion of damages evidence before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent cases face increased likelihood of damages theories reaching the jury, even where there are significant methodological challenges to the plaintiff's expert analysis.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after having missed the deadline for seeking a stay of its original declaratory judgment action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent cannot circumvent the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay under § 1659(a) by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action. BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled DJ action after missing the 30-day deadline in the original action, preventing gamesmanship in coordinating parallel ITC and district court proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies filing declaratory judgment actions in patent disputes can avoid indefinite stays by opponents who attempt to manipulate statutory deadlines through procedural gamesmanship involving refiled actions.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents must carefully track and timely assert their right to a mandatory stay of parallel district court actions, as the deadline cannot be reset by dismissing and refiling the district court case.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB on when to exercise discretion to deny IPR institution (such as the Fintiv and NHK-Spring frameworks) are general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that these instructions do not bind the Director and that non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify IPR institution guidance without the burden of formal rulemaking, enabling faster policy responses to evolving patent litigation dynamics.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking IPR as a cost-effective alternative to district court litigation face continued uncertainty from discretionary denials governed by PTO guidance that is not subject to public comment or judicial challenge on substantive grounds.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class settlement of claims that PACER fees exceeded those authorized by statute, finding the settlement fair and the attorneys' fees and incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives proper. Hundreds of thousands of PACER users will be fully or substantially reimbursed for fees paid during the eight-year class period.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses, law firms, and organizations that use PACER will receive reimbursement of overcharged fees, and the decision validates incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives, encouraging organizational plaintiffs to serve as class representatives.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade remedy proceedings is incompatible with the statute and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic competitors, and the public gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire data from public access regardless of whether it genuinely qualifies as confidential.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit sensitive business information in ITC questionnaires face increased risk of public disclosure unless they affirmatively demonstrate the information qualifies for confidential treatment under the statute.
